text.title: testing-backend-languages Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for testing-backend-languages. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Ruby tests MUST use RSpec (not Minitest) with let blocks for test data and factory_bot factories for object graphs. Tests MUST be free of slow I/O unless explicitly labelled :integration.
rule.rationale: RSpec's expressive DSL and factory_bot are the Rails community standard for service-layer tests. Minitest is valid for simpler unit tests but agents should default to RSpec unless the Gemfile shows minitest as the project runner.

rule.statement: Laravel tests MUST use Pest function syntax with RefreshDatabase for any test touching the database. HTTP endpoint tests MUST use Laravel's $this->postJson / getJson assertion chain rather than raw Guzzle or curl.
rule.rationale: Pest is the Laravel default and produces cleaner output than PHPUnit class syntax. RefreshDatabase wraps each test in a transaction to avoid state leak. The built-in JSON assertion methods check status and structure in one call.

rule.statement: Spring Boot unit and slice tests MUST NOT use @SpringBootTest unless the test genuinely requires the full application context. Service tests MUST use plain unit tests with Mockito. Repository tests MUST use @DataJpaTest. Controller tests MUST use @WebMvcTest.
rule.rationale: @SpringBootTest adds 5-30 seconds of context startup to every test class that uses it. Test slices boot only the relevant layers; controller slices are 10x faster than full context for endpoint tests.

rule.statement: .NET service tests MUST use xUnit [Fact] / [Theory] with Moq (or NSubstitute) for dependencies and FluentAssertions for assertions. Tests MUST NOT use WebApplicationFactory unless testing HTTP middleware or integration scenarios.
rule.rationale: xUnit is the .NET standard. FluentAssertions error messages name the expected and actual values clearly, reducing debug time. WebApplicationFactory boots the full ASP.NET pipeline — reserve it for integration tests.

rule.statement: Rust unit tests MUST use #[test] in a #[cfg(test)] mod tests block in the same file as the implementation. Async tests MUST use #[tokio::test] with the tokio runtime — never mix #[test] with async fn without a runtime wrapper. Integration tests MUST live in tests/ and MUST NOT use #[cfg(test)].
rule.rationale: Mixing #[test] and async fn without a runtime wrapper compiles but panics at runtime with "no runtime found". The #[cfg(test)] guard ensures test code is never compiled into production binaries.
